items.title: Music in training sets is the new Spotify Wrapped: how genAI uses your music
items.excerpt: Producers are discovering their own music inside AI training sets via The Atlantic's new searchable AI Watchdog database, which covers four datasets totalling over 21 million tracks—Berlin artist Hainbach found 151 of his songs in a single LAION YouTube corpus. CDM examines what the exposure means for musicians' rights and creative autonomy.

items.title: Investigation by The Atlantic reveals many millions of songs used for AI music training
items.excerpt: The Atlantic published four searchable databases of music used to train AI models—two holding 12 million and 9 million tracks respectively—including hit songs from Taylor Swift and Bad Bunny alongside tens of thousands of independent artists, with the databases now being used as evidence in active copyright litigation against Suno and Udio.

items.title: Artists can now check whether their music has been used to train AI
items.excerpt: Mixmag covers The Atlantic's AI Watchdog searchable interface, which lets any artist enter their name to see which songs appear in the major AI music training datasets; companies often claimed to use only 'freely available' online content, but the databases reveal the true scale of copyrighted material actually accessed.

items.title: Over 21 million copyrighted songs are circulating among AI developers, watchdog tool launched
items.excerpt: DJ Mag reports that The Atlantic's AI Watchdog investigation uncovered four major datasets—containing more than 21 million tracks combined—being shared among AI developers for model training, with a new public search tool allowing artists to identify whether their recordings appear in training pipelines used by Suno and Udio.

items.title: Traxsource launches AI transparency labels
items.excerpt: Electronic music download platform Traxsource will begin labelling tracks as 'human-made', 'AI-assisted' or removing fully AI-generated music from July 1, working with detection partners SH Labs and SoundPatrol; cofounder Brian Tappert says the goal is to ensure value stays with human-created works while acknowledging AI is not going away.

items.title: AI Training Data 2026: The Atlantic Reveals Whose Music Ended Up in Suno and Udio
items.excerpt: GearNews breaks down The Atlantic's AI Watchdog report for producers, explaining how the four searchable datasets—including a 12-million-track LAION YouTube corpus—expose exactly which recordings were fed into Suno and Udio's training pipelines and how AI models learned to imitate genre, timbre, and style from real productions.

items.title: With Latent Terrain, crack open AI and explore neural synthesis in Max
items.excerpt: Latent Terrain is a free, open-source Max/MSP external by researcher Jasper Zheng that turns pre-trained RAVE neural audio autoencoder models into a playable real-time synthesis instrument—producers navigate AI latent space using their own sounds as input, with all processing running locally and no internet connection required.

items.title: The Atlantic uncovers 'millions' of songs used for AI training
items.excerpt: Music Ally covers The Atlantic's AI Watchdog investigation, noting the two largest datasets contain 12 million and 9 million tracks, and explains that AI companies often claimed to use only freely available online content while the databases reveal the true scale of copyrighted material accessed for model training.
